content: Hi all! Made some small fixes to docs while familiarizing myself with the code base. Would appreciate it a lot if you could my PR a look before I submit the patches to the mailing list. Thanks! :)
I'd say that 17 commits for the size of the PR is a bit too much, but each commit does seem to be unique, so squashing makes no sense. Other than that everything is good.
There are four different "fix typo" commits, they can be squashed.
Thanks, I will squash those typo fixes before submitting!

Two questions regarding the email address I use to send the patches:

  1. Does it have to be subscribed to the mailing lists I send the patches to?
  2. Does it have to be the same address that I configured in git (i.e., the one in the Signed-of-by tags and commit metadata)
I'm pretty sure that your email will be rejected if you are not subscribed. (as an anti-spam measure)
And it's best to use the same email if at all possible.

I'm pretty sure that your email will be rejected if you are not subscribed. (as an anti-spam measure)

I've read in the "good-first-issues" on GitHub that patches should be send to the LKML as well. I would like to avoid subscribing to that. It seems that most patches here are not send to the LKML though. Is it Ok if I just send to the rust-for-linux list?
I'm only subscribed to rust-for-linux, so that will probably work.
I don't have any issue sending to both when only susbscribed to rust-for-linux.
You can use b4 to send patches via the HTTP endpoint https://b4.docs.kernel.org/en/latest/contributor/send.html
Then you don't have to worry about your outgoing email server messing up your patches

I'm only subscribed to rust-for-linux, so that will probably work.

I don't have any issue sending to both when only susbscribed to rust-for-linux.

Thanks, then I'll send to both.

Andreas Hindborg said:

You can use b4 to send patches via the HTTP endpoint https://b4.docs.kernel.org/en/latest/contributor/send.html

Never tried b4, I always used git send-mail. Is it any better/easier?

Andreas Hindborg: You can use b4 to send patches via the HTTP endpoint https://b4.docs.kernel.org/en/latest/contributor/send.html

Andreas Hindborg: Then you don't have to worry about your outgoing email server messing up your patches

Good to know, thanks
Oh, no, it looks like I ran into a ratelimit on my smtp server provider :/ Any advise on fixing that? Also should I resend the complete series or only the parts that were blocked?
If you setup the proper In-Reply-To header (--in-reply-to=20240116160141.165951-1-kernel@valentinobst.de), you can send only the remaining ones if you wish.
Thanks! I actually checked the ratelimit beforehand and it is 500/h but still they seem to dislike the fast pace.

My pleasure! In that case, perhaps the web submission endpoint would help you.

In any case, do not worry. It happens :)
Do I still need to specify all of the --cc and --to then I use --in-reply-to?
And should I add --no-thread?
Okay, looks like the rate limit persists. I'll then use the web endpoint and resubmit everything.

Valentin Obst said:

Thanks! I actually checked the ratelimit beforehand and it is 500/h but still they seem to dislike the fast pace.

This is another reason to use b4 :) Bypass all that fun.
Is this why the list only got 1..3 of 13? :smile:
You can still add the others without resending everything, just make sure the in-reply-to is set to the cover letter's ID (--in-reply-to=20240116160141.165951-1-kernel@valentinobst.de)

Is this why the list only got 1..3 of 13? :smile:

It is, sorry for the inconvenience.

You can still add the others without resending everything, just make sure the in-reply-to is set to the cover letter's ID (--in-reply-to=20240116160141.165951-1-kernel@valentinobst.de)

For this series I decided to stick with smtp as I want to avoid reposting and abandoning the half finished series out there. Looks like I can send 4 mails/h. Thus you should have it all in your inbox in 2h.
Those of you who already gave feedback on Github might also find the changelog there useful.
4 emails per hour? Wow, that's quite restrictive
Is it because of poor infrastructure of your mail provider?

50 mails/h, but each patch goes to 12 recipients. I was assuming that the rate limit was by contract age not address age (the mail hosting contract is very old, the address was created today), which lead me to believe the limit is 500/h.

If you need to resend this, you are probably OK not directly CCing all the reviewers since everyone is on the RFL list. Doesn't hurt to make it explicit if you can, but it's not worth making it take you 8 hours to send a patchset...
The lists and the three maintainers are the most important. Looks like you got yourself on the CC list as well, not sure if that counts against your limit

The lists and the three maintainers are the most important. Looks like you got yourself on the CC list as well, not sure if that counts against your limit

Tried that but still forgot to tell git not so cc the reviewers found in the body :/

https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20240123150112.124084-1-kernel@valentinobst.de/

The other half comes in an hour or so.
:smile: you have some bad email luck

If it makes things easier, I think it is okay to send patches from a different email than your signed-off-bys as long as you can verify they are both you. I think Ariel might have gone through something like this with difficulty using a cisco email

Tried that but still forgot to tell git not so cc the reviewers found in the body :/

It sounds to me like you are at the point of starting to remove people you would have Cc'd normally, or perhaps even considering splitting series in several just in case and so on. Please instead move to b4's endpoint to avoid that trouble.

Please instead move to b4's endpoint to avoid that trouble.

yes, should have just followed Andreas' advise, no reason not to. Won't happen again.
No worries & thanks! If the endpoint does not work for you, please let us know.

Seems like it worked ... however, there's one last pitfall that I almost stepped into: If an smtp server is configured, b4 will prefer it over the web endpoint. There's a commit that adds a flag to control this but is is not yet in the latest version.
